title: An AI-Native Protocol and Ecosystem for Computational Earth and Space Science
author: Wu, Koutian
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Computational models are central to Earth and space science, from land surface hydrology and atmospheric dynamics to ocean circulation and space weather prediction. Yet their development and use remain confined to small communities of specialists who can navigate codebases of 200,000 or more lines of Fortran and C. The vast majority of domain scientists, those with deep expertise in hydrology, biogeochemistry, atmospheric dynamics, cryosphere physics, space plasma physics, or related fields, lack the software engineering skills to modify, configure, or confidently run these models. As a result, they are restricted to qualitative observations rather than the rigorous quantitative simulations their research demands. We propose two complementary responses. First, a simple protocol that constrains AI-assisted model development by inheriting the hierarchical, process-oriented workflow already practiced in physical model work: grounded repository tracing, component-level tests, conservation and bounds checks, and mandatory expert review before acceptance of AI-mediated modifications. Second, an AI-native ecosystem that allows domain scientists to design, execute, and analyze simulations entirely through natural language interaction with AI coding agents, removing the software engineering barrier without compromising scientific rigor. The protocol and ecosystem are illustrated using Earth System Models but apply to any computational science codebase where physical correctness must be preserved: weather prediction, ocean modeling, space weather simulation, hydrology, and beyond.

Yet their development and use remain confined to small communities of specialists who can navigate codebases of 200,000 or more lines of Fortran and C. The vast majority of domain scientists, those with deep expertise in hydrology, biogeochemistry, atmospheric dynamics, cryosphere physics, space plasma physics, or related fields, lack the software engineering skills to modify, configure, or confidently run these models. As a result, they are restricted to qualitative observations rather than the rigorous quantitative simulations their research demands. We propose two complementary responses. First, a simple protocol that constrains AI-assisted model development by inheriting the hierarchical, process-oriented workflow already practiced in physical model work: grounded repository tracing, component-level tests, conservation and bounds checks, and mandatory expert review before acceptance of AI-mediated modifications.
